coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent composite based on peptide nanotubes activating coumarin 6 for sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fang
Tang, Rong
Kang, Yujie
Cui, Xiaoyan
Wang, Ya
Yang, Xiupei
coredata.dc:description: New coccine (NC), as a kind of common colorant, has been frequently used in our daily life. Herein, the fluorescent composite (PNTs@C6) prepared by the hydrophobic non-covalent interaction between peptide nanotubes and coumarin 6 (C6) was designed for the determination of NC. Due to the activation of C6 by peptide nanotubes, the composite exhibits strong green fluorescence emission, which can be selectively quenched by NC through the inner filter effect. Therefore, a new fluorescent method based on the PNTs@C6 composite for NC detection was constructed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching of the sensor exhibits a good linear relationship with the concentration of NC in the range of 0.01–10 μM and the limit of detection is 3.6 nM. Furthermore, the strategy shows simplicity, rapid response and high selectivity and has been successfully applied to the detection of NC in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peptide nanotube
Coumarin 6
Fluorescent composite
New coccine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of NC in real sample
Fluorescence performance of PNTs@C6 and the sensing mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Doctoral Scientific Research Start-up Foundation of China West Normal University
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21906129 , 22276150 ) and the Doctoral Scientific Research Start-up Foundation of China West Normal University ( 18Q027 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of PNTs (a) and PNTs@C6 (b). (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of C6, PNTs, PNTs@C6 in water and C6 in methanol. (d) FTIR spectra of C6, PNTs, PNTs@C6.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of C6, PNTs@C6 in water, C6 in methanol and a mixture of PNTs@C6 and NC. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of NC, PNTs@C6, mixture of PNTs@C6 and NC. (c) Fluorescence lifetime of PNTs@C6 without and with NC, respectively. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of NC and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of the PNTs@C6.
The influence of the amount of PNTs@C6 (a), pH (b) and the incubation time for PNTs@C6 and NC (c) on the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PNTs@C6 after addition of various concentrations of NC (inset: concentrations of NC were 0,0.01,0.1,0.2,0.4,0.6, 0.8,1,1.5,2,3,4,6,8,10 µM, respectively). (b) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of NC (0.01–10 µM).

(a) Selectivity of PNTs@C6 for detection of NC over interfering substances (NC, sunset yellow and amaranth: 2 μM. Tartrazine, curcumin and brilliant blue: 8 μM. Other interfering substances: 20 μM). (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the other interfering substances and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of the PNTs@C6.
Diagram illustration for the fabrication of PNTs@C6 and its application in detecting NC in food samples.
The application of determination of NC in food samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent composite based on peptide nanotubes activating coumarin 6 for sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanchong Food and Drug Inspection Institute, Nanchong 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanchong Food and Drug Inspection Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanchong Food and Drug Inspection Institute, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New coccine (NC), as a kind of common colorant, has been frequently used in our daily life. Herein, the fluorescent composite (PNTs@C6) prepared by the hydrophobic non-covalent interaction between peptide nanotubes and coumarin 6 (C6) was designed for the determination of NC. Due to the activation of C6 by peptide nanotubes, the composite exhibits strong green fluorescence emission, which can be selectively quenched by NC through the inner filter effect. Therefore, a new fluorescent method based on the PNTs@C6 composite for NC detection was constructed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching of the sensor exhibits a good linear relationship with the concentration of NC in the range of 0.01–10 μM and the limit of detection is 3.6 nM. Furthermore, the strategy shows simplicity, rapid response and high selectivity and has been successfully applied to the detection of NC in food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colorants have been used in food manufacturing to make food more attractive and to bring people a good taste experience. Compared with natural colorants, synthetic colorants have the advantages of excellent color uniformity, low price, more stability and less fading, and have been widely used in the food industry[1]. However, excessive intake of synthetic colorants may cause a threat to human health[2]. New coccine (NC, also called as E 124, Acid red 18, Ponceau 4R or cochineal red A), one of strong acid dyes, is commonly used as food colorants, but excessive consumption of NC may lead to severe mutagenic and carcinogenic effects[3,4]. Many countries have banned the use of NC in any foods, such as the United States, Denmark and so on[5]. In some other countries, it is permitted as a food colorants and have a strict addition level in certain food products such as beverages, pickles, and candies[6]. In China, the range of maximum allowable limit for NC in the food industry is 0.05–0.5 g/kg (GB2760-2014). The acceptable daily intake for NC is 0–4 mg/kg established by Agriculture Organization (FAO) and World Health Organization (WHO) Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA, 2019) [7,8]. To ensure the food quality and safety of consumers, it is essential to establish a rapid, simple, accurate and sensitive method for NC detection in food products.

To date, common analytical methods for the determination of NC include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)[9–11], capillary electrophoresis (CE)[12], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy (SERS)[6,13], electrochemical analysis[14–20]. Nevertheless, many of these methods have limitations, such as expensive instruments, time consumption, extensive sample pretreatment processes and cumbersome operation, preventing their further widespread application. By comparison, fluorescence analysis is more convenient, effective and flexible, and has been used to construct NC detection methods, where fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon QDs, silicon QDs, CdTe QD are involved as signal elements[7,21–24]. It is of interest to prepare novel fluorescent materials with excellent properties by using a simpler method for NC analysis, which can help to further improve the performance of the method and expand its practicality.

Recently, inspired by the process of protein misfolding to form β-amyloid deposits in Alzheimer's disease, a large of researchers have focused on using amyloid proteins as templates to construct highly ordered nanostructures [25–28]. There are several types of noncovalent interactions associated with the self-assembly process, such as hydrogen bonding, solvent interaction, hydrophobic interaction, van der Waals force, electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking and metal coordination[29–32]. The short peptide fragment 17LVFF20 derived from β-amyloid protein is the core motif driving the self-assembly[33]. The peptide sequence Ac-KLVFFAL-NH2 (KL-7) as a derivative sequence of 17LVFF20 has the characteristic of antiparallel β-sheet signature and can assemble to form tubular structures in a 4:6 (v/v) mixture of acetonitrile/water solution [34,35]. A large number of lysine residues on the surface of nanotubes enables multifunctional surface modification, while the positively charged surface and hydrophobic walls of the nanotubes can bind some molecules through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions[28,34,36]. It is well known that most fluorescent small molecules, due to their hydrophobic structure, tend to aggregate in aqueous solution and cause fluorescence quenching[37]. Therefore, complex steps are usually necessary to modify their structure or composite with other materials to improve their water solubility and luminescent properties[37–39]. In this way, the self-assembled peptide nanotubes can precisely provide the hydrophobic environment and large surface area for these molecules, which provides a new idea for the construction of novel fluorescent materials based on peptide self-assembly materials.

Herein, the fluorescent composite (PNTs@C6) was prepared through the strong hydrophobic interaction between self-assembled peptide nanotubes (PNTs) and the fluorescent small molecule coumarin 6 (C6) by simple mixing. Due to the activation of C6 by the PNTs, the composite exhibits strong green fluorescence emission. In addition, the composite also shows good water dispersibility and stability. The addition of NC was found to quench the fluorescence of the material, and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was further explored. Based on this, PNTs@C6 was used as a fluorescent sensor to construct a novel method for NC detection with high sensitivity and selectivity, which was further applied to analyze NC in real food samples (Scheme 1 ). This approach, which has the advantage of being simple to prepare, easy to manipulate and with good detection performance, not only expands the application of peptide self-assembled fluorescent composites, but also provides a new approach to NC detection.

Ethanol (≥99.7 %), methanol (≥99.5 %), glucose (AR, 96.0 %), L-Ascorbic acid (VC, ≥99.7 %), anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥96.0 %) and potassium nitrate (KNO3, ≥99.0 %) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Sucrose was obtained from Chongqing Maoye Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Chongqing, China). New coccine (≥98.0 %) was purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Acetonitrile (≥99.9 %) was obtained from Chengdu Nuoershi Technology Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China).Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, ≥99.0 %) was purchased from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Coumarin 6 (>98.0 %), sodium metaphosphate (AR), sodium benzoate (99.0 %), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD, 98.0 %), Vitamin B1 (VB1, 99.0 %), lactose (AR), sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O, 99.0 %), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4·12H2O, 99.0 %), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O,99.0 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), barium nitrate (Ba(NO3)2, 99.0 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 99.0 %), aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O, 99.9 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co. (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in this experiment are of analytical grade or of the best commercially available grade. Buffer made with 10 mM NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 solution (PBS) was used to adjust the pH value of the solution. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ· cm was used throughout this work.

Fluorescence spectra were performed with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was recorded by a Nicolet 6700 Fourier in situ infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-2550 ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence decays were recorded by Edinburgh FLS1000 (UK). Transmission electron microscope images were measured by HT7700 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was TD-3500 (Dandong Tongda Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China). Raman spectra were detected at a WITec alpha300 access (Germany).

Ac-KLVFFAL-NH2 peptide was dissolved in acetonitrile /water (1:1, v/v) solution containing 1 % trifluoroacetic acid and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH. Then, the final solution concentration was 3.5 mM. Then, it was sonicated to clarification. Finally, it was self-assembled at 37 ℃ for 4 days to form a gel-like solution [38,40].

C6 (3.5 mg) was completely dissolved in 100 mL methanol with a final concentration of 100 μM. Then, 90 μL of C6 were added to 200 μL of 3.5 mM PNTs solution and incubated for 6 h at 15 ℃ in a shaking chamber [41]. Finally, the solution containing PNTs@C6 was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3500 Da molecular weight cut off) in 10 % methanol solution for 3 h. After centrifugation, the obtained PNTs@C6 was dispersed in water and stored at 4 ℃ under dark.

For NC fluorometric measurement, 41 μL of the obtained PNTs@C6 stock solution and 15 μL of 100 mM PBS (pH=8) buffer solution were mixed with 79 μL ultrapure water. Ultimately, the concentration of PNTs in the solution was 0.40 mM. Then, 15 μL of NC with different concentrations was added and kept at ambient temperature for 10 min. After that, all the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 466 nm.

Miranda orange juice drink, candy and fruit jam were purchased from the local market as food samples. The Miranda soft drink was ultrasonically treated for 30 min to remove CO2 and then filtered with a 0.45 µm filter membrane. A total of 5.0 g of crushed candy was accurately weighed and dissolved in 10 mL hot water, with a micro-filter (0.45 μm) to remove large particles. For fruit jam, 3.0 g of fruit jam was extracted with 10 mL of methanol/water (1:1, v/v) by sonication for 10 min, and then centrifuged (11000 rpm) for 10 min. Extraction was repeated three times and the solutions were filtered using a 0.45 µm filter membrane[7]. All of the collected filtrate was stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NC in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In the assay, 34 μL of PNTs@C6 solution, 15 μL of 100 mM PBS (pH=8) buffer solution, and 15 μL processed real samples were mixed with 71 μL ultrapure water and kept at room temperature for 10 min. Spiked samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of NC to the food samples. After the pretreatment, the samples were analyzed following a similar procedure as described above.

PNTs@C6 was obtained by directly mixing self-assembled PNTs with C6, incubating for a period of time followed by dialysis (Scheme 1). The PNTs were prepared by self-assembly of short peptide KL-7 according to previous reports [33,42]. The obtained PNTs have a uniform tubular shape, as shown in Fig. 1
a, with a diameter of approximately 40–50 nm (counting at least 100 nanotubes) and a length of up to several micrometers, in agreement with the literature. After loading C6, the resulting PNTs@C6 still maintains the intact morphology of nanotubes (Fig. 1b), indicating that the loading of C6 through hydrophobic interaction will not affect their structure. Besides, the hollow structure of the nanotubes can be clearly seen from the fracture surface of PNTs@C6 possibly caused by processes such as centrifugation (Fig. S2a), further proving the formation of the nanotubes self-assembled from KL-7. For C6, its methanol solution is bright yellow-green with characteristic absorption at about 460 nm (Fig. 1c). If water is used as the solvent, the solution is dark yellow with almost no absorption in the UV–vis range, as the strong hydrophobicity of C6 makes it insoluble in water and suppresses its optical properties. After loading, however, PNTs@C6 dispersed well in water and the absorption band at approximately 460 nm reappeared. This not only proves the successful loading of C6 on PNTs and the successful preparation of PNTs@C6, but also demonstrates that PNTs provide a perfect hydrophobic environment for C6, which lays a foundation for the recovery of its optical properties in aqueous solution.

The FT-IR and XPS characterizations were then performed. As shown in Fig. 1d, the IR spectrum of PNTs contains a strong amide-I stretching band at 1623 cm−1 and a weak band at 1690 cm−1, which is typical of antiparallel β-sheets [43]. The peak at 1550 cm−1 arises from N-H in-plane bending or C-N stretching modes associated with the amide-II band. The peak at 1410 cm−1 may be due to the deformation of the characteristic peak of CH2 at 1410–1460 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 3270, 3030 and 2960 cm−1 are related to the amide-based absorption, the stretching vibration of the benzene ring and the stretching of C–H bond, respectively [44]. For C6, the absorption peaks at 1710, 1620 and 1410 cm−1 are attributed to the C = O stretching band and the C-N absorption band [45]. The FT-IR spectrum of PNTs@C6 retains the N–H and C–H stretching vibration absorption from PNTs and C6 at approximately 3600–3200 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1, respectively. The carbonyl stretching vibration peaks from the amide or lactone of PNTs and C6 at 1620, 1690, 1550 cm−1 are also preserved [38,43,45]. Moreover, PNTs@C6 also has a similar peak pattern to C6 in the fingerprint region (1500–600 cm−1). The XPS spectrum (Fig. S1a) further shows that PNTs@C6 contains elements C, O, N and a small amount of S, and the species of these elements have been analyzed by peak splitting as shown in Fig. S1b-e. The high-resolution spectra of C1s of PNTs@C6 (Fig. S1b) reveal the presence of C = C (283.9 eV), C–C (284.8 eV), C-N/C-S (285.8 eV), C-O (286.4 eV), and C = O/C = N bonds (288.0 eV). The high-resolution spectra of O1s (Fig. S1c) contains three peaks of C-OH (530.8 eV), C = O (531.4 eV) and O-C-O bonds (532.5 eV). The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. S2d) exhibits three peaks of C = N (398.5 eV), C-N-C (399.8 eV) and N–H bonds (401.3 eV). And the high-resolution spectra of S2p (Fig. S2e) can be fitted into two peaks of C-S = N (163.9 eV) and C-S-C bonds (168.5 eV) [46,47]. Similar to FT-IR and XPS results, the element mapping and EDX spectrum also indicate the presence of a large amount of C, O, N elements and a small amount of S element in PNTs@C6, as shown in Fig. S2b-e.

In addition, 1H NMR spectra, XRD and Raman spectra were also characterized. Fig. S3 shows typical 1H NMR spectra of C6, PNTs and PNTs@C6. As the hydrogen atom environments of C6 and PNTs are very similar, some characteristic peaks of their 1H NMR spectra overlap to some extent. After loading C6 on the PNTs, however, most of the characteristic peaks of the PNTs are retained, while the doublet at 7.63 ppm splits into a triplet and the characteristic peak of C6 at 2.4 ppm appears [36,45]. The XRD pattern of PNTs (Fig. S4a) exhibits diffraction peaks at 5.3° and 8.2°, characteristic of the orthogonal peptide spacing in β-strands and β-sheets, respectively, for cross-β assemblies [43]. In comparison, the diffraction signal of PNTs is weakened with C6 loaded. Fig. S4b shows the Raman spectra of PNTs and PNTs@C6. The Raman characteristic peaks of PNTs could be clearly observed at 1001 and 1669 cm−1 [48,49]. ​After loading C6, however, the strong fluorescence of PNTs@C6 interferes with the Raman test, leading to a strong background signal that obscures the Raman signal, indirectly proving the successful loading of C6 on the PNTs. The results above together confirm that C6 has been successfully supported on PNTs, and PNTs@C6 was obtained as expected.

According to previous reports, KL-7 self-assembles to form bilayer peptide nanotubes with a cavity structure and a positively charged surface [33,42,50–53]. The wall of PNTs is formed by the anti-parallel out-of-register arrangement of the hydrophobic 17LVFFA21 sequence from β-amyloid. The presence of hydrophobic regions on the wall of PNTs enables them to bind a variety of hydrophobic small molecules and activate their properties that are inhibited in water, thereby constructing various functional materials [33,40,42,53–57]. Similarly, the fluorescence of hydrophobic C6 is almost completely suppressed in water and it can be easily loaded onto PNTs by simple mixing, as demonstrated by the above characterizations, and subsequent experiments (Fig. 2a) show that its fluorescence is restored, all in agreement with the literatures above. Therefore, hydrophobic interaction is considered the most dominant interaction between PNTs and C6. In addition, the cavity of PNTs can facilitate the embedding of small molecules into the wall, and the π-π interaction exists between the benzene rings of PNTs and C6, all of which promote the loading of C6 and the stability of the PNTs@C6 material [52]. With multiple non-covalent interactions including hydrophobic interaction, embedding and π-π interaction, C6 is successfully loaded onto PNTs and the fluorescence is activated, which lays a foundation for the construction of PNTs@C6 based fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of PNTs@C6 and the sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The emission properties were then tested in view of the good dispersion and recovery of the absorption of PNTs@C6 in aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 2a. The methanol solution of C6 shows green fluorescence at 504 nm, while the aqueous solution of C6 has almost no fluorescence emission, indicating that the fluorescence of strongly hydrophobic C6 in water is completely suppressed, in agreement with the results of the UV–vis spectra presented above. However, the composite PNTs@C6 dispersed in water exhibits strong emission, as expected at 500 nm [58], and its solution shows bright green fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation. This suggests that the PNTs can provide a hydrophobic microenvironment similar to that of methanol for C6 and thus activate the fluorescence of C6 in aqueous solution. Further experiments show that the obtained PNTs@C6 not only have strong emission and can be well dispersed in aqueous solution, but can also maintain the stability of fluorescence for at least 21 days in aqueous solution (Fig. S2). Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 2a that the addition of NC can significantly quench the fluorescence of PNTs@C6. All of these results offer the potential for further use of PNTs@C6 as a fluorescent sensor for NC detection.

Then, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of PNTs@C6 by NC was investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and fluorescence lifetime experiments. As shown in Fig. 2b, after adding NC to the material, no other new absorption peaks appear except the characteristic absorption peak of NC, indicating that there is no static composite formation between PNTs@C6 and NC. The fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 2c) of PNTs@C6 without NC and in the presence of NC is 2.82 ns and 2.76 ns, respectively, suggesting that the addition of NC has little effect on the fluorescence lifetime of PNTs@C6. These results indicate that fluorescence quenching is neither caused by the formation of nonfluorescent static composites nor by the transfer of excited electrons or energy. As shown in Fig. 2d, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NC overlaps well with the fluorescence excitation and emission peak of PNTs@C6, fully in line with the inner filter effect (IFE) [59,60]. All the results above demonstrate that the fluorescence of PNTs@C6 is significantly quenched by NC through IFE.

Considering the fluorescence quenching of PNTs@C6 by NC, a fluorescent method for NC detection was constructed based on PNTs@C6. To obtain better measurement properties, experimental conditions such as the amount of PNTs@C6, pH value of the system and incubation time were optimized. First, the amount of PNTs@C6 was investigated as depicted in Fig. 3a. When the amount of PNTs@C6 increases from 0.24 to 0.72 mM, the response of the system to NC (the ratio of F0/F) gradually decreases, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after adding NC, respectively. Considering that the initial fluorescence intensity of the system itself is low at low concentrations of PNTs@C6, this may affect the sensitivity and linear range of the method. The final chosen material dose of 0.4 mM ensures not only a strong initial fluorescence intensity of the system but also the response of the system to NC. In addition, we also performed the titration experiment for NC detection using another material dosage of 0.32 mM with low initial fluorescence intensity but high NC response, as shown in Fig. S3, whose linear range (0.01–5 μM) and detection limit (4.1 nM) were not as good as the experiment with 0.4 mM material dosage. The influence of pH on detection is shown in Fig. 3b. The fluorescence of PNTs@C6 itself does not change much as pH increases from 4 to 10. However, the response of the material to NC (F0/F) increased gradually, which may be due to the fact that more sulfonate dissociation of NC under alkaline conditions enhances the interaction with PNTs@C6. Considering the response to NC and the measurement of real samples, pH 8 was chosen for subsequent experiments. The effect of reaction time was studied as illustrated in Fig. 3c. As the reaction time increases, the F0/F ratio increases rapidly, reaching a maximum at 10 min, after which it remains essentially constant. In summary, 0.4 mM of PNTs@C6, pH 8 and the reaction time of 10 min were set as the optimal experimental conditions.

Under the abovementioned conditions, the sensing method was evaluated by recording the change in fluorescence intensity of PNTs@C6 after incubation with different concentrations of NC. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of PNTs@C6 at 500 nm declines gradually with increasing NC concentration. More significantly, a good linear relationship is obtained between the ratio of F0/F and the concentration of NC in the range of 0.01–10 μM (R2 = 0.9933). It can be also clearly seen from the photos under the ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 4 a, inset) that the green fluorescence of the system is gradually quenched with increasing NC concentration, proving the potential of the method for visual detection of NC. The limit of detection (LOD) was further calculated to be 3.6 nM, which was comparable to or even lower than the previously reported methods (Table S1) [6,7,10–13,61,62]. At the same time, free C6 has also been used to detect NC in methanol as solvent, as shown in Fig. S5a-b, with the linear range for NC detection of 5–90 µM. The detection limit is 1.5 µM, which is nearly 3 orders of magnitude larger than that of PNTs@C6. As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S5c, both in water and in an aqueous solution containing 10 % methanol, the fluorescence of free C6 is too weak for sensor construction. Thus, the loading of C6 on PNTs not only activates the suppressed fluorescence of C6 in water, but also enables the sensitive detection of NC in aqueous solution with the constructed PNTs@C6.

In addition, the specificity of PNTs@C6 sensor to NC was tested by interference assay, in which potential interfering substances such as Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, lactose, glucose, sucrose, β-cyclodextrin, sodium benzoate, sodium metaphosphate sunset yellow, tartrazine, amaranth, curcumin and brilliant blue were involved. It is evident from Fig. 5a that the fluorescence signal of the detection system is hardly affected even when the concentration of these substances is as high as 10 times that of NC. It can also be seen from the photographs in the inset that the fluorescence of the system is significantly quenched only after the addition of NC (the second tube from left to right). We further measured the UV–vis absorption spectra of these substances, as shown in Fig. 5b. Unlike the absorption spectra of NC, the absorption spectra of these species substantially do not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of PNTs@C6. This is the reason why the fluorescence of PNTs@C6 can be selectively quenched by NC. The results above clearly indicate that the PNTs@C6 sensor with high sensitivity and good selectivity can be used for the detection of NC in complex environments.

To validate the feasibility of the proposed method, candy, mirinda soft drink, and fruit jam were selected as real samples. The mirinda soft drink was diluted 10 times before measurement. The results show that the concentrations of NC in candy, mirinda soft drink and fruit jam are 0.48, 0.67 and 1.17 μM, respectively. Recovery experiments were also carried out on real samples by adding various concentrations of NC for further analysis. As listed in Table 1 . satisfactory recoveries of 93.7 % to 109.2 % were achieved and RSDs were not more than 2.63 %, which suggests that the reported method has good reliability and practicability for the determination of new coccine in real samples.

In summary, a fluorescence assay for NC was developed based on the inner filter effect between NC and PNTs@C6. The PNTs@C6 composite is prepared by simple mixing of self-assembled PNTs with C6 through strong hydrophobic interactions. The loading of C6 onto PNTs not only activates the fluorescence of C6 but also improves the water dispersibility and stability of the material. Efficient and selective quenching of PNTs@C6 fluorescence by NC through IFE enables the high sensitivity and selectivity of NC detection with PNTs@C6-based fluorescence sensors, which have been successfully applied to NC detection in real food samples. This strategy, with its convenient material preparation, easy operation and good detection performance, not only expands the application of peptide self-assembled fluorescent composite materials but also provides a new approach to NC detection.

Fang Li conceived the concept, designed and performed all the experiments, and wrote the original draft. Rong Tang, Yujie Kang and Xiaoyan Cui: revised it critically for important intellectual content. Xiupei Yang and Ya Wang: funding acquisition, conceived the primary idea, revised it critically for important intellectual content, editing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21906129, 22276150) and the Doctoral Scientific Research Start-up Foundation of China West Normal University (18Q027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123492.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of 40 synthetic food colors in drinks and candies by high-performance liquid chromatography using a short column with photodiode array detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Yoshioka, K. Ichihashi, Determination of 40 synthetic food colors in drinks and candies by high-performance liquid chromatography using a short column with photodiode array detection, Talanta, 74 (2008) 1408-1413.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly reproducible and sensitive silver nanorod array for the rapid detection of Allura Red in candy, Spectrochim. Acta. A Mol. Biomol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yao, W. Wang, K. Tian, W.M. Ingram, J. Cheng, L. Qu, H. Li, C. Han, Highly reproducible and sensitive silver nanorod array for the rapid detection of Allura Red in candy, Spectrochim. Acta. A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc., 195 (2018) 165-171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence-quenching method for quantitative analysis of ponceau 4R in beverage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, L. Na, Y. Jiang, D. Han, D. Lou, L. Jin, A fluorescence-quenching method for quantitative analysis of ponceau 4R in beverage, Food Chem., 221 (2017) 803-808.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA damage induced by red food dyes orally adminstered to pregnant and male mice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tsuda, M. Murakami, N. Matsusaka, K. Kano, K. Taniguchi, Y. Sasaki, DNA damage induced by red food dyes orally adminstered to pregnant and male mice, Toxicol. Sci., 61 (2001) 92-99.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biopolymer based electrochemical sensor for ponceau 4R: an insight into electrochemical kinetics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.T. Cyriac, U. Sivasankaran, K.G. Kumar, Biopolymer based electrochemical sensor for ponceau 4R: an insight into electrochemical kinetics, J. Electrochem. Soc., 165 (2018) B746-B752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical calculation (DFT), raman and surface-enhanced raman scattering (SERS) study of ponceau 4R
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xie, Y. Li, Y. Sun, H. Wang, H. Qian, W. Yao, Theoretical calculation (DFT), raman and surface-enhanced raman scattering (SERS) study of ponceau 4R, Spectrochim. Acta. A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc., 96 (2012) 600-604.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free fluorescence nanosensor based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots for ultra-sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Liu, Z. Mi, X. Huo, L. Yuan, Y. Bao, Z. Liu, F. Feng, A label-free fluorescence nanosensor based on nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots for ultra-sensitive detection of new coccine in food samples, Food Chem., 368 (2022) 130829.
M. Leulescu, G. Iacobescu, M. Bojan, P. Rotaru, Ponceau 4R azoic red dye, Therm. Anal. Calorim., 138 (2019) 2091-2101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-N, N'-methylene bisacrylamide) monolithic column embedded with gamma-alumina nanoparticles microextraction coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography for the determination of synthetic food dyes in soft drink samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Li, X. Zhou, S.S. Tong, Q. Jia, Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-N,N'-methylene bisacrylamide) monolithic column embedded with gamma-alumina nanoparticles microextraction coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography for the determination of synthetic food dyes in soft drink samples, Talanta, 105 (2013) 386-392.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of 20 synthetic dyes in chili powders and syrup-preserved fruits by liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.F. Tsai, C.H. Kuo, D.Y. Shih, Determination of 20 synthetic dyes in chili powders and syrup-preserved fruits by liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry, J. Food Drug Anal., 23 (2015) 453-462.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a green chromatographic method for simultaneous determination of food colorants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Khanavi, M. Hajimahmoodi, A.M. Ranjbar, M.R. Oveisi, M.R.S. Ardekani, G. Mogaddam, Development of a green chromatographic method for simultaneous determination of food colorants, Food Anal. Methods., 5 (2011) 408-415.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive determination of erythrosine and other red food colorants using capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ryvolova, P. Taborsky, P. Vrabel, P. Krasensky, J. Preisler, Sensitive determination of erythrosine and other red food colorants using capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection, J. Chromatogr. A., 1141 (2007) 206-211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic nanoparticles on metal-organic framework: A versatile SERS platform for adsorptive detection of new coccine and orange II dyes in food

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wu, H. Pu, L. Huang, D. W. Sun, Plasmonic nanoparticles on metal-organic framework: A versatile SERS platform for adsorptive detection of new coccine and orange II dyes in food, Food Chem., 328 (2020) 127105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The potential of electrochemistry for one-pot and sensitive analysis of patent blue V, tartrazine, acid violet 7 and ponceau 4R in foodstuffs using IL/Cu-BTC MOF modified sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Darabi, M. Shabani-Nooshabadi, H. Karimi-Maleh, A. Gholami, The potential of electrochemistry for one-pot and sensitive analysis of patent blue V, tartrazine, acid violet 7 and ponceau 4R in foodstuffs using IL/Cu-BTC MOF modified sensor, Food Chem., 368 (2022) 130811.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trace analysis of ponceau 4R in soft drinks using differential pulse stripping voltammetry at SWCNTs composite electrodes based on PEDOT:PSS derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, H. Zhang, Z. Wang, J. Zhang, X. Duan, J. Xu, Y. Wen, Trace analysis of ponceau 4R in soft drinks using differential pulse stripping voltammetry at SWCNTs composite electrodes based on PEDOT:PSS derivatives, Food Chem., 180 (2015) 186-193.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly-sensitive and rapid detection of ponceau 4R and tartrazine in drinks using alumina microfibers-based electrochemical sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, L. Hu, X. Liu, B. Liu, K. Wu, Highly-sensitive and rapid detection of ponceau 4R and tartrazine in drinks using alumina microfibers-based electrochemical sensor, Food Chem., 166 (2015) 352-357.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-wall carbon nanotube film-based electrochemical sensor for rapid detection of Ponceau 4R and Allura Red

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, X.J. Zhang, X.H. Lu, J.Q. Yang, K.B. Wu, Multi-wall carbon nanotube film-based electrochemical sensor for rapid detection of Ponceau 4R and Allura Red, Food Chem., 122 (2010) 909-913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of the isomers of ponceau 4R and amaranth using an expanded graphite paste electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, M. Wang, C. Shentu, W. Wang, Z. Chen, Simultaneous determination of the isomers of ponceau 4R and amaranth using an expanded graphite paste electrode, Food Chem., 160 (2014) 11-15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of carmoisine, allura red and ponceau 4R in sweets and soft drinks by differential pulse polarography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chanlon, L. Joly-Pottuz, M. Chatelut, O. Vittori, J.L. Cretier, Determination of carmoisine, allura red and ponceau 4R in sweets and soft drinks by differential pulse polarography, J. Food Compos. Anal., 18 (2005) 503-515.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Combeau, M. Chatelut, O. Vittori, Identification and simultaneous determination of azorubin,allura red and ponceau 4R by differential pulse polarography: Application to soft drinks, Talanta, 56 (2008) 115-122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: F-doped silicon quantum dots as a novel fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative detection of new coccine and application in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, M.H. Zan, L. Cao, J.H. Peng, P.Y. Wang, X.P. Pang, Y. Zhang, L. Li, Q. Mei, W.F. Dong, F-doped silicon quantum dots as a novel fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative detection of new coccine and application in food samples, Microchem. J., 179 (.2022) 107453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mohapatra, S. Sahu, N. Sinha, S.K. Bhutia, Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells, Analyst, 140 (2015) 1221-1228.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay for carbendazim based on N-doped carbon quantum dots and gold nanocluster nanohybrid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, X. Xing, T. Zou, Z. Wang, R. Zhao, P. Hong, S. Peng, X. Zhang, Y. Wang, A novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay for carbendazim based on N-doped carbon quantum dots and gold nanocluster nanohybrid, J. Hazard. Mater., 386 (2020) 121958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-off-on fluorescent nanosensing: Materials, detection strategies and recent food applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Y. He, D.W. Sun, Z.H. Wu, H.B. Pu, Q.Y. Wei, On-off-on fluorescent nanosensing: Materials, detection strategies and recent food applications, Trends Food Sci. Technol., 119 (2022) 243-256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Endogenous reactive oxygen species-triggered morphology transformation for enhanced cooperative interaction with mitochondria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.B. Cheng, X.H. Zhang, Y.J. Gao, L. Ji, D. Hou, Z. Wang, W. Xu, Z.Y. Qiao, H. Wang, Endogenous reactive oxygen species-triggered morphology transformation for enhanced cooperative interaction with mitochondria, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 141 (2019) 7235-7239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Folic acid derived hydrogel enhances the survival and promotes therapeutic efficacy of iPS cells for acute myocardial infarction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, J. Gao, Y. Shang, Y. Hua, M. Ye, Z. Yang, C. Ou, M. Chen, Folic acid derived hydrogel enhances the survival and promotes therapeutic efficacy of iPS cells for acute myocardial infarction, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 10 (2018) 24459-24468.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, R. Peltier, M. Zhang, D. Song, H. Huang, G. Chen, Y. Chen, F. Zhou, Q. Hao, L. Bian, M.L. He, Z. Wang, Y. Hu, H. Sun, Desuccinylation-triggered peptide self-assembly: Live cell imaging of SIRT5 activity and mitochondrial activity modulation, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 142 (2020) 18150-18159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glycopeptide nanofiber platform for abeta-sialic acid interaction analysis and highly sensitive detection of abeta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Lei, R. Geng, Z. Xu, Y. Dang, X. Hu, L. Li, P. Geng, Y. Tian, W. Zhang, Glycopeptide nanofiber platform for abeta-sialic acid interaction analysis and highly sensitive detection of abeta, Anal. Chem., 91 (2019) 8129-8136.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lai, F.L. Li, Z.F. Zou, M. Saeed, Z.A. Xu, H.J. Yu, Bio-inspired amyloid polypeptides:From self-assembly to nanostructure design and biotechnological applications, Appl. Mater. Today, 22 (2021) 100966.
